title: Yashvi Mittal
text: Hi! I’m Yashvi Mittal, a Master’s student at Baruch College. This site collects my coursework and mini-projects for STA 9750 (Software Tools for Data Analysis), Fall 2025.

title: Yashvi Mittal
text:
Projects

Mini-Project: The Electoral College — analysis of U.S. presidential and House election data, 1976-2024.
Mini-Project: The Ultimate Playlist — data-driven playlist curation from song characteristics and user-generated playlists.
Individual Project: CUNY Retirement Plan Analysis — Monte Carlo decision support for new CUNY faculty choosing between retirement plans.








Last Updated: Friday 05 22, 2026 at 18:47PM

title: STA 9750 Mini-Project #02: The Business of Show Business
text:
Hollywood development is a portfolio problem: find a concept with broad appeal, attach credible talent, and reduce uncertainty by learning from prior successes. This report uses IMDb’s non-commercial datasets to identify durable genres, successful personnel, and a classic title suitable for a contemporary remake.


Show code
options(repos = c(CRAN = "https://cloud.r-project.org"))
pkgs <- c("tidyverse", "scales", "DT", "knitr", "stringr")
missing_pkgs <- pkgs[!vapply(pkgs, requireNamespace, logical(1), quietly = TRUE)]
if (length(missing_pkgs) > 0) install.packages(missing_pkgs)

library(tidyverse)
library(scales)
library(DT)
library(knitr)
library(stringr)

dir.create("data/mp02", recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)
knitr::opts_chunk$set(cache.lazy = FALSE)

title: STA 9750 Mini-Project #02: The Business of Show Business
text:
Task 1: Column Type Correction


Show code
NAME_BASICS <- NAME_BASICS |>
  mutate(birthYear = as.numeric(birthYear), deathYear = as.numeric(deathYear))

TITLE_BASICS <- TITLE_BASICS |>
  mutate(
    isAdult = as.logical(as.integer(isAdult)),
    startYear = as.numeric(startYear),
    endYear = as.numeric(endYear),
    runtimeMinutes = as.numeric(runtimeMinutes)
  )

TITLE_EPISODES <- TITLE_EPISODES |>
  mutate(seasonNumber = as.numeric(seasonNumber), episodeNumber = as.numeric(episodeNumber))

TITLE_RATINGS <- TITLE_RATINGS |>
  mutate(averageRating = as.numeric(averageRating), numVotes = as.numeric(numVotes))

TITLE_PRINCIPALS <- TITLE_PRINCIPALS |>
  mutate(ordering = as.numeric(ordering))




Show code
list(
  NAME_BASICS = NAME_BASICS,
  TITLE_BASICS = TITLE_BASICS,
  TITLE_EPISODES = TITLE_EPISODES,
  TITLE_RATINGS = TITLE_RATINGS,
  TITLE_CREW = TITLE_CREW,
  TITLE_PRINCIPALS = TITLE_PRINCIPALS
) |>
  imap(function(tbl, nm) {
    cat("\n\n### ", nm, "\n\n", sep = "")
    print(glimpse(tbl))
  })

title: STA 9750 Mini-Project #02: The Business of Show Business
text:
Task 2: Instructor-Provided Questions


Show code
title_counts <- TITLE_BASICS |>
  filter(titleType %in% c("movie", "tvSeries", "tvEpisode")) |>
  count(titleType, name = "n") |>
  arrange(desc(n))

oldest_living <- NAME_BASICS |>
  filter(!is.na(birthYear), is.na(deathYear)) |>
  arrange(birthYear) |>
  slice_head(n = 10) |>
  select(primaryName, birthYear, primaryProfession, knownForTitles)

perfect_episode <- TITLE_BASICS |>
  inner_join(TITLE_RATINGS, join_by(tconst)) |>
  filter(titleType == "tvEpisode", averageRating == 10, numVotes >= 200000) |>
  left_join(TITLE_EPISODES, join_by(tconst)) |>
  left_join(
    TITLE_BASICS |> select(parentTconst = tconst, seriesTitle = primaryTitle, seriesStart = startYear),
    join_by(parentTconst)
  ) |>
  select(primaryTitle, seriesTitle, seasonNumber, episodeNumber, averageRating, numVotes)

mark_hamill_known_for <- NAME_BASICS |>
  filter(primaryName == "Mark Hamill") |>
  separate_longer_delim(knownForTitles, ",") |>
  left_join(TITLE_BASICS, join_by(knownForTitles == tconst)) |>
  select(primaryName, primaryTitle, titleType, startYear, genres)

best_series_12_plus <- TITLE_EPISODES |>
  inner_join(TITLE_RATINGS, join_by(tconst)) |>
  group_by(parentTconst) |>
  summarize(
    episodes = n(),
    avg_episode_rating = weighted.mean(averageRating, numVotes, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_votes = sum(numVotes, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  filter(episodes > 12) |>
  inner_join(TITLE_BASICS, join_by(parentTconst == tconst)) |>
  arrange(desc(avg_episode_rating), desc(total_votes)) |>
  select(primaryTitle, startYear, episodes, avg_episode_rating, total_votes) |>
  slice_head(n = 10)

happy_days <- TITLE_BASICS |>
  filter(primaryTitle == "Happy Days", titleType == "tvSeries", startYear == 1974) |>
  slice_head(n = 1)

happy_days_by_season <- TITLE_EPISODES |>
  filter(parentTconst == happy_days$tconst[1]) |>
  inner_join(TITLE_RATINGS, join_by(tconst)) |>
  group_by(seasonNumber) |>
  summarize(
    episodes = n(),
    average_rating = weighted.mean(averageRating, numVotes, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  filter(!is.na(seasonNumber))

Show code
happy_days_by_season |>
  ggplot(aes(x = seasonNumber, y = average_rating)) +
  geom_line(color = "#2c7fb8", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(color = "#2c7fb8", size = 2) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = sort(unique(happy_days_by_season$seasonNumber))) +
  labs(
    title = "Happy Days Ratings by Season",
    subtitle = "Weighted by episode vote count",
    x = "Season",
    y = "Average IMDb rating"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()









The season trend provides the empirical version of the “jump the shark” claim. If later seasons sit below the early seasons, the idiom matches the crowd ratings; if the line is flatter, the phrase is more culturally memorable than statistically dramatic.

title: STA 9750 Mini-Project #02: The Business of Show Business
text:
Task 3: Custom Success Metric
I define success as a blend of quality and audience reach:
success_score = averageRating * log10(numVotes + 1)
This keeps the rating scale meaningful while preventing vote count alone from dominating.


Show code
TITLE_RATINGS <- TITLE_RATINGS |>
  mutate(success_score = averageRating * log10(numVotes + 1))

movie_success <- TITLE_BASICS |>
  filter(titleType == "movie", !is.na(startYear), !isAdult) |>
  inner_join(TITLE_RATINGS, join_by(tconst)) |>
  mutate(decade = floor(startYear / 10) * 10)

top_success_movies <- movie_success |>
  slice_max(success_score, n = 10) |>
  select(primaryTitle, startYear, genres, averageRating, numVotes, success_score)

popular_low_quality <- movie_success |>
  filter(numVotes >= quantile(numVotes, 0.99, na.rm = TRUE)) |>
  slice_min(success_score, n = 5) |>
  select(primaryTitle, startYear, genres, averageRating, numVotes, success_score)

prestige_person <- "Christopher Nolan"
prestige_projects <- NAME_BASICS |>
  filter(primaryName == prestige_person) |>
  separate_longer_delim(knownForTitles, ",") |>
  left_join(movie_success, join_by(knownForTitles == tconst)) |>
  arrange(desc(success_score)) |>
  select(primaryName, primaryTitle, startYear, genres, averageRating, numVotes, success_score)

recent_solid_check <- movie_success |>
  filter(startYear >= 2010, numVotes >= 100000) |>
  summarize(
    median_success = median(success_score, na.rm = TRUE),
    p75_success = quantile(success_score, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE),
    p90_success = quantile(success_score, 0.90, na.rm = TRUE)
  )

success_threshold <- quantile(movie_success$success_score, 0.90, na.rm = TRUE)

title: STA 9750 Mini-Project #02: The Business of Show Business
section: Task 4: Trends in Success Over Time

title: STA 9750 Mini-Project #02: The Business of Show Business
text:
Task 5: Key Personnel


Show code
principals_with_names <- TITLE_PRINCIPALS |>
  filter(category %in% c("actor", "actress", "director")) |>
  inner_join(NAME_BASICS, join_by(nconst)) |>
  inner_join(movie_success |> select(tconst, primaryTitle, startYear, genres, success_score, averageRating, numVotes), join_by(tconst))

actor_candidates <- principals_with_names |>
  filter(category %in% c("actor", "actress"), str_detect(genres, fixed(selected_genre))) |>
  group_by(nconst, primaryName) |>
  summarize(
    genre_projects = n_distinct(tconst),
    genre_successes = n_distinct(tconst[success_score >= success_threshold]),
    best_success = max(success_score, na.rm = TRUE),
    known_for = first(knownForTitles),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  filter(genre_projects >= 2, genre_successes >= 1) |>
  arrange(desc(genre_successes), desc(best_success)) |>
  slice_head(n = 10)

director_candidates <- TITLE_CREW |>
  separate_longer_delim(directors, ",") |>
  rename(nconst = directors) |>
  inner_join(NAME_BASICS, join_by(nconst)) |>
  inner_join(movie_success |> filter(str_detect(genres, fixed(selected_genre))), join_by(tconst)) |>
  group_by(nconst, primaryName) |>
  summarize(
    genre_projects = n_distinct(tconst),
    genre_successes = n_distinct(tconst[success_score >= success_threshold]),
    best_success = max(success_score, na.rm = TRUE),
    known_for = first(knownForTitles),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  filter(genre_projects >= 1, genre_successes >= 1) |>
  arrange(desc(genre_successes), desc(best_success)) |>
  slice_head(n = 10)

target_actors <- actor_candidates |> slice_head(n = 2)
target_director <- director_candidates |> slice_head(n = 1)

title: STA 9750 Mini-Project #02: The Business of Show Business
section: Task 6: Finding a Classic Movie to Remake
text:
Task 6: Finding a Classic Movie to Remake


Show code
recent_titles <- movie_success |>
  filter(startYear >= max(startYear, na.rm = TRUE) - 25) |>
  select(recent_title = primaryTitle)

classic_candidates <- movie_success |>
  filter(
    str_detect(genres, fixed(selected_genre)),
    startYear <= 1999,
    numVotes >= quantile(numVotes, 0.90, na.rm = TRUE),
    averageRating >= 7.0
  ) |>
  anti_join(recent_titles, join_by(primaryTitle == recent_title)) |>
  arrange(desc(success_score)) |>
  slice_head(n = 10)

classic_choice <- classic_candidates |> slice_head(n = 1)

classic_personnel <- bind_rows(
  TITLE_CREW |>
    filter(tconst == classic_choice$tconst[1]) |>
    separate_longer_delim(directors, ",") |>
    transmute(nconst = directors, original_role = "director"),
  TITLE_CREW |>
    filter(tconst == classic_choice$tconst[1]) |>
    separate_longer_delim(writers, ",") |>
    transmute(nconst = writers, original_role = "writer"),
  TITLE_PRINCIPALS |>
    filter(tconst == classic_choice$tconst[1], category %in% c("actor", "actress")) |>
    transmute(nconst, original_role = category)
) |>
  distinct() |>
  left_join(NAME_BASICS, join_by(nconst)) |>
  mutate(alive = is.na(deathYear)) |>
  select(primaryName, original_role, birthYear, deathYear, alive, primaryProfession)

title: STA 9750 Mini-Project #04: Exploring Recent US Political Shifts
text:
This report studies county-level shifts from the 2020 to 2024 U.S. presidential elections. The pipeline follows the assignment constraint: county results are scraped from Wikipedia rather than imported from an election package, and county shapes come from the U.S. Census Bureau.


Show code
options(repos = c(CRAN = "https://cloud.r-project.org"))
pkgs <- c("tidyverse", "httr2", "rvest", "sf", "scales", "ggrepel", "knitr", "maps")
missing_pkgs <- pkgs[!vapply(pkgs, requireNamespace, logical(1), quietly = TRUE)]
if (length(missing_pkgs) > 0) install.packages(missing_pkgs)

library(tidyverse)
library(httr2)
library(rvest)
library(sf)
library(scales)
library(ggrepel)
library(knitr)
library(maps)

dir.create("data/mp04", recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)
theme_set(theme_minimal())

title: STA 9750 Mini-Project #04: Exploring Recent US Political Shifts
STA 9750 Mini-Project #04: Exploring Recent US Political Shifts
STA 9750 Mini-Project #04: Exploring Recent US Political Shifts
text:
Task 4: Initial Analysis Questions


Show code
q1 <- election_shift |> slice_max(rep_votes_2024, n = 5)
q2 <- election_shift |> slice_max(dem_share_2020, n = 5)
q3 <- election_shift |> slice_max(trump_abs_shift, n = 5)
q4 <- election_shift |>
  group_by(state) |>
  summarize(
    rep_2024 = sum(rep_votes_2024, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_2024 = sum(total_votes_2024, na.rm = TRUE),
    rep_2020 = sum(rep_votes_2020, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_2020 = sum(total_votes_2020, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  mutate(rep_shift = rep_2024 / total_2024 - rep_2020 / total_2020, harris_shift = -rep_shift) |>
  slice_max(harris_shift, n = 5)
q5 <- election_shift |> slice_max(area_sq_km, n = 5)
q6 <- election_shift |> slice_max(voter_density_2020, n = 5)
q7 <- election_shift |> slice_max(turnout_change, n = 5)

answer_table <- bind_rows(
  q1 |> transmute(question = "Most Trump votes in 2024", state, county_name, value = comma(rep_votes_2024)),
  q2 |> transmute(question = "Highest Biden share in 2020", state, county_name, value = percent(dem_share_2020, accuracy = 0.01)),
  q3 |> transmute(question = "Largest absolute Trump vote increase", state, county_name, value = comma(trump_abs_shift)),
  q4 |> transmute(question = "Largest Harris / smallest Trump shift", state, county_name = "All counties", value = percent(harris_shift, accuracy = 0.01)),
  q5 |> transmute(question = "Largest county by area", state, county_name, value = comma(area_sq_km)),
  q6 |> transmute(question = "Highest 2020 voter density", state, county_name, value = comma(voter_density_2020)),
  q7 |> transmute(question = "Largest turnout increase", state, county_name, value = comma(turnout_change))
)

title: STA 9750 Mini-Project #04: Exploring Recent US Political Shifts
text:
Task 5: County Shift Map
The map uses county centroids and arrows. Rightward shifts point east; leftward shifts point west. Alaska and Hawaii are shifted and rescaled into a more reasonable position so the map keeps all states while remaining legible.


Show code
state_base <- map_data("state")

shift_shapes <- county_shapes |>
  left_join(election_shift, join_by(county_fips)) |>
  filter(!is.na(trump_share_shift))

centroids <- shift_shapes |>
  st_transform(4326) |>
  st_point_on_surface() |>
  mutate(
    x = st_coordinates(geometry)[, 1],
    y = st_coordinates(geometry)[, 2],
    x = case_when(
      STATEFP == "02" ~ x * 0.35 - 95,
      STATEFP == "15" ~ x + 52,
      TRUE ~ x
    ),
    y = case_when(
      STATEFP == "02" ~ y * 0.35 + 16,
      STATEFP == "15" ~ y + 5,
      TRUE ~ y
    ),
    xend = x + trump_share_shift * 4,
    yend = y,
    direction = if_else(trump_share_shift >= 0, "Rightward", "Leftward")
  ) |>
  st_drop_geometry()




Show code
par(mar = c(0, 0, 3, 0))
map("state", fill = TRUE, col = "grey96", border = "white", lwd = 0.2)
segment_colors <- if_else(centroids$direction == "Rightward", "#d95f0e99", "#2c7fb899")
segments(
  centroids$x,
  centroids$y,
  centroids$xend,
  centroids$yend,
  col = segment_colors,
  lwd = 0.5
)
title("County-Level Presidential Vote Shift, 2020 to 2024")
legend(
  "bottomleft",
  legend = c("Rightward", "Leftward"),
  col = c("#d95f0e", "#2c7fb8"),
  lwd = 2,
  bty = "n"
)

title: STA 9750 Mini-Project #04: Exploring Recent US Political Shifts
section: Task 6: Partisan Talking Points and Statistical Tests
text:
Task 6: Partisan Talking Points and Statistical Tests
For this section I adopt the Democratic persona suggested by the assignment: Trump won, but the victory can be framed as narrower than the county map makes it appear.


Show code
weighted_shift <- with(election_shift, weighted.mean(trump_share_shift, total_votes_2024, na.rm = TRUE))
median_shift <- median(election_shift$trump_share_shift, na.rm = TRUE)

set.seed(9750)
n_sims <- 200

bootstrap_median_shift <- replicate(
  n_sims,
  median(sample(election_shift$trump_share_shift, replace = TRUE), na.rm = TRUE)
)

bootstrap_weighted_shift <- replicate(
  n_sims,
  {
    boot_index <- sample.int(nrow(election_shift), nrow(election_shift), replace = TRUE)
    weighted.mean(
      election_shift$trump_share_shift[boot_index],
      election_shift$total_votes_2024[boot_index],
      na.rm = TRUE
    )
  }
)

median_shift_test <- tibble(
  test = "Bootstrap test: median county Trump shift is at least five points",
  estimate = median_shift,
  null_value = 0.05,
  p_value = mean(bootstrap_median_shift >= 0.05)
)

weighted_shift_test <- tibble(
  test = "Bootstrap test: vote-weighted Trump shift is at least five points",
  estimate = weighted_shift,
  null_value = 0.05,
  p_value = mean(bootstrap_weighted_shift >= 0.05)
)

shift_by_density <- election_shift |>
  mutate(density_group = ntile(voter_density_2020, 5)) |>
  group_by(density_group) |>
  summarize(
    counties = n(),
    median_density = median(voter_density_2020, na.rm = TRUE),
    weighted_trump_shift = weighted.mean(trump_share_shift, total_votes_2024, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_votes_2024 = sum(total_votes_2024, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

state_shift <- election_shift |>
  group_by(state) |>
  summarize(
    trump_shift = weighted.mean(trump_share_shift, total_votes_2024, na.rm = TRUE),
    votes_2024 = sum(total_votes_2024, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )




Show code
election_shift |>
  ggplot(aes(x = trump_share_shift)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 50, fill = "#d95f0e", color = "white") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0.05, linetype = "dashed") +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = percent) +
  labs(
    title = "Most County Shifts Were Not a Five-Point Earthquake",
    x = "Trump share shift, 2020 to 2024",
    y = "Counties"
  )









Show code
shift_by_density |>
  ggplot(aes(x = density_group, y = weighted_trump_shift)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#2c7fb8") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent) +
  labs(
    title = "The Vote-Weighted Shift Looks Smaller in Dense Counties",
    x = "2020 voter-density quintile",
    y = "Weighted Trump share shift"
  )









Show code
state_shift |>
  slice_max(abs(trump_shift), n = 15) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(state, trump_shift), y = trump_shift, fill = trump_shift > 0)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(`TRUE` = "#d95f0e", `FALSE` = "#2c7fb8")) +
  labs(
    title = "State Shifts Varied Widely",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Vote-weighted Trump shift"
  )











Show code
tibble(
  test = c(median_shift_test$test, weighted_shift_test$test),
  estimate = c(median_shift_test$estimate, weighted_shift_test$estimate),
  null_value = c(median_shift_test$null_value, weighted_shift_test$null_value),
  p_value = c(median_shift_test$p_value, weighted_shift_test$p_value)
) |>
  kable(digits = 4)











test
estimate
null_value
p_value




Bootstrap test: median county Trump shift is at least five points
0.0157
0.05
0


Bootstrap test: vote-weighted Trump shift is at least five points
0.0158
0.05
0

title: STA 9750 Mini-Project #04: Exploring Recent US Political Shifts
text:
Deliverable: Partisan Talking Points
Do not let a red county map do the talking for the country. Land does not vote; people do. The 2024 result moved toward Trump, but the evidence does not require the grandiose conclusion that America entered a new political era. The average county map is visually dramatic because thousands of low-population counties cover enormous space. When shifts are weighted by actual 2024 votes, the national movement is 1.6%, while the median county shift is 1.6%. That is a real change, but not a blank check for mythmaking.
The density analysis makes the point sharper. Dense counties, where more voters are packed into less geography, tell a quieter story than the wall-to-wall map of county arrows. The t-test against a five-point county shift also asks the question directly: was this really a sweeping five-point county earthquake? The answer is in the table above, and it is more modest than the loudest post-election narrative.
So the Democratic argument is simple: Trump won the rules of the contest, but the visual drama overstates the mandate. The country shifted, yet unevenly. A disciplined opposition should study where turnout fell, where persuasion failed, and where small margins were allowed to become a national story.

title: STA 9750 Mini-Project #01: Fiscal Characteristics of Major US Public Transit Systems
STA 9750 Mini-Project #01: Fiscal Characteristics of Major US Public Transit Systems
text:
Task 1: Creating Syntactic Names


Show code
USAGE <- USAGE |>
  rename(metro_area = `UZA Name`)

title: STA 9750 Mini-Project #01: Fiscal Characteristics of Major US Public Transit Systems
text:
Task 2: Recoding Transit Modes


Show code
USAGE |>
  distinct(Mode) |>
  arrange(Mode) |>
  kable(col.names = "Original NTD mode codes")





Original NTD mode codes




AR


CB


CC


CR


DR


FB


HR


IP


LR


MB


MG


PB


RB


SR


TB


TR


VP


YR





Show code
mode_labels <- c(
  AG = "Automated Guideway",
  AR = "Alaska Railroad",
  CB = "Commuter Bus",
  CC = "Cable Car",
  CR = "Commuter Rail",
  DR = "Demand Response",
  DT = "Demand Response Taxi",
  FB = "Ferryboat",
  HR = "Heavy Rail",
  IP = "Inclined Plane",
  LR = "Light Rail",
  MB = "Bus",
  MG = "Monorail/Automated Guideway",
  PB = "Publico",
  RB = "Bus Rapid Transit",
  SR = "Streetcar",
  TB = "Trolleybus",
  VP = "Vanpool",
  YR = "Hybrid Rail"
)

USAGE <- USAGE |>
  mutate(Mode = recode(Mode, !!!mode_labels, .default = "Unknown"))




Show code
USAGE |>
  select(-`NTD ID`, -`3 Mode`) |>
  rename(unlinked_passenger_trips = UPT, vehicle_revenue_miles = VRM) |>
  sample_n(min(1000, nrow(USAGE))) |>
  mutate(month = as.character(month)) |>
  datatable(options = list(pageLength = 10, scrollX = TRUE))

title: STA 9750 Mini-Project #01: Fiscal Characteristics of Major US Public Transit Systems
STA 9750 Mini-Project #01: Fiscal Characteristics of Major US Public Transit Systems
text:
Task 4: Three Additional Transit Facts


Show code
latest_month <- max(USAGE$month, na.rm = TRUE)

top_metros_latest <- USAGE |>
  filter(month == latest_month) |>
  group_by(metro_area) |>
  summarize(trips = sum(UPT, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") |>
  slice_max(trips, n = 10)

rail_bus_mix <- USAGE |>
  filter(year(month) == 2024) |>
  mutate(category = if_else(Mode %in% c("Heavy Rail", "Commuter Rail", "Light Rail", "Streetcar"), "Rail", "Non-rail")) |>
  group_by(metro_area, category) |>
  summarize(trips = sum(UPT, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") |>
  group_by(metro_area) |>
  mutate(total_trips = sum(trips), rail_share = if_else(category == "Rail", trips / total_trips, NA_real_)) |>
  ungroup() |>
  filter(total_trips >= 10000000, category == "Rail") |>
  slice_max(rail_share, n = 10)

service_intensity <- USAGE |>
  filter(year(month) == 2024) |>
  group_by(Agency, Mode) |>
  summarize(UPT = sum(UPT, na.rm = TRUE), VRM = sum(VRM, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") |>
  filter(VRM > 0, UPT >= 1000000) |>
  mutate(trips_per_vrm = UPT / VRM) |>
  slice_max(trips_per_vrm, n = 10)

top_metros_latest |>
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(metro_area, trips), y = trips)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#2c7fb8") +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +
  labs(
    title = "Largest Transit Markets in the Latest Available Month",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Unlinked passenger trips",
    caption = paste("Latest month:", format(latest_month, "%B %Y"))
  ) +
  theme_minimal()











Show code
cat("### Fact 1: The largest transit markets dominate national ridership\n")

title: STA 9750 Mini-Project #01: Fiscal Characteristics of Major US Public Transit Systems
text:
Task 5: Table Summarization


Show code
USAGE_2022_ANNUAL <- USAGE |>
  filter(year(month) == 2022) |>
  group_by(`NTD ID`, Agency, metro_area, Mode) |>
  summarize(UPT = sum(UPT, na.rm = TRUE), VRM = sum(VRM, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

USAGE_AND_FINANCIALS <- left_join(
  USAGE_2022_ANNUAL,
  FINANCIALS,
  join_by(`NTD ID`, Mode)
) |>
  drop_na() |>
  mutate(
    farebox_recovery = `Total Fares` / Expenses,
    expense_per_upt = Expenses / UPT,
    fare_per_upt = `Total Fares` / UPT,
    expense_per_vrm = Expenses / VRM,
    fare_per_vrm = `Total Fares` / VRM
  )

USAGE_2022_ANNUAL |>
  slice_max(UPT, n = 10) |>
  kable(digits = 2)

title: STA 9750 Mini-Project #01: Fiscal Characteristics of Major US Public Transit Systems
section: Task 6: Farebox Recovery Among Major Systems
text:
Task 6: Farebox Recovery Among Major Systems
I define a major system as any agency-mode pair with at least 400,000 annual unlinked passenger trips. This threshold removes tiny systems whose ratios can swing wildly from one accounting entry.


Show code
major_systems <- USAGE_AND_FINANCIALS |>
  filter(UPT >= 400000, VRM > 0, Expenses > 0)

summary_questions <- bind_rows(
  major_systems |> slice_max(UPT, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) |> mutate(metric = "Most UPT in 2022"),
  major_systems |> slice_max(farebox_recovery, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) |> mutate(metric = "Highest farebox recovery"),
  major_systems |> slice_min(expense_per_upt, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) |> mutate(metric = "Lowest expenses per UPT"),
  major_systems |> slice_max(fare_per_upt, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) |> mutate(metric = "Highest total fares per UPT"),
  major_systems |> slice_min(expense_per_vrm, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) |> mutate(metric = "Lowest expenses per VRM"),
  major_systems |> slice_max(fare_per_vrm, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) |> mutate(metric = "Highest total fares per VRM")
) |>
  select(metric, Agency, Mode, metro_area, UPT, VRM, `Total Fares`, Expenses, farebox_recovery, expense_per_upt, fare_per_upt, expense_per_vrm, fare_per_vrm)

summary_questions |>
  mutate(across(c(UPT, VRM, `Total Fares`, Expenses), comma)) |>
  mutate(across(contains("per_"), dollar)) |>
  mutate(farebox_recovery = percent(farebox_recovery, accuracy = 0.1)) |>
  kable()




















metric
Agency
Mode
metro_area
UPT
VRM
Total Fares
Expenses
farebox_recovery
expense_per_upt
fare_per_upt
expense_per_vrm
fare_per_vrm









Show code
major_systems |>
  filter(UPT >= quantile(UPT, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = expense_per_upt, y = farebox_recovery, size = UPT, color = Mode)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.75) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = dollar) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent) +
  scale_size_continuous(labels = comma) +
  labs(
    title = "Farebox Recovery Rewards Both Ridership and Fare Revenue",
    subtitle = "Major 2022 agency-mode systems",
    x = "Operating expense per unlinked passenger trip",
    y = "Farebox recovery ratio",
    size = "UPT",
    color = "Mode"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
